prompt: Under 49 CFR Part 830, what is the regulatory distinction between an 'aircraft accident' and an 'aircraft incident'? Provide the key definitional criteria.
gold_decision: An aircraft accident (49 CFR §830.2) is an occurrence associated with aircraft operation in which any person suffers death or serious injury, OR the aircraft receives substantial damage. An aircraft incident is an occurrence other than an accident associated with aircraft operation that affects or could affect continued safe operation. The critical distinction is injury severity OR damage threshold: accidents require death, serious injury (hospitalization >48h, bone fracture except simple finger/toe/nose, severe hemorrhage, nerve/muscle/tendon damage, internal organ damage, 2nd/3rd degree burns, or infective illness), or substantial aircraft damage. Incidents are near-misses and safety-relevant occurrences that fall short of those thresholds.
required_safety_constraints: 49 CFR Part 830 governs NTSB accident/incident notification and reporting
Substantial damage excludes engine failure/damage if only one engine fails on multi-engine aircraft, bent fairings/cowlings, small punctures, ground damage to rotor blades, and dents/scratches
Fatal injuries result in death within 30 days of the accident
acceptable_variants: Answer may emphasize injury definition detail or damage threshold detail as primary distinguisher
Answer may note 'serious injury' as defined list rather than generic phrase

provenance.generation_rule: Definitional knowledge card: question derived from 49 CFR Part 830 statutory text; no scenario data; answer drawn from regulatory definitions section.

prompt: What are the NTSB immediate notification requirements under 49 CFR Part 830 §830.5? List the specific accident and incident types that trigger immediate notification.
gold_decision: Operators must immediately notify the nearest NTSB field office when: ACCIDENTS — any aircraft accident. INCIDENTS requiring immediate notification: flight control system malfunction/failure; inability of any required flight crew member to perform normal flight duties due to incapacitation; turbine engine failure (other than reverse thrust) that requires use of emergency procedure; in-flight fire; aircraft collision in flight; damage to property (other than aircraft) estimated at $25,000 or more; for large multiengine aircraft (>12,500 lb): in-flight failure of electrical/hydraulic system requiring use of emergency checklist; in-flight loss of 1/3 or more of elevator/aileron/rudder effectiveness; any event where NTSB determines notification is necessary. 'Immediately' means as soon as practicable.
required_safety_constraints: Immediate NTSB notification is mandatory — not discretionary — for all accidents
The operator, not the pilot-in-command, bears primary notification responsibility
5-day written report required within 10 days for accidents and for incidents involving in-flight crew incapacitation only
acceptable_variants: Answer may list items in different order
Answer may note the property damage dollar threshold as approximate or subject to update

provenance.generation_rule: Regulatory knowledge card: question derived from 49 CFR §830.5 statutory text; no scenario data.

prompt: Explain the NTSB probable cause determination process. What is the difference between a 'probable cause' and a 'contributing factor' in NTSB accident reports?
gold_decision: The NTSB determines the probable cause(s) of an accident as the finding(s) that most directly led to the accident — it is the primary causal factor without which the accident would not have occurred as it did. Contributing factors are conditions, events, or deficiencies that increased the risk or likelihood of the accident but were not the direct proximate cause. The NTSB uses a 'findings' methodology where each finding represents a causal or contributing element. Probable cause language typically uses 'the pilot's failure to...' or 'the malfunction of...', while contributing factors may include weather, fatigue, maintenance history, or organizational factors. NTSB does NOT assign legal blame — findings are investigative conclusions for safety improvement, not fault determination.
required_safety_constraints: NTSB accident reports are investigative documents, not legal findings of fault (49 USC §1154)
Probable cause is limited to factors within the scope of the NTSB investigation
Multiple probable causes may be listed; one does not exclude others
acceptable_variants: Answer may use 'proximate cause' as synonym for probable cause
Answer may emphasize the systemic/organizational factors that often appear as contributing factors
evidence_requirements: 49 USC §1154 (non-admissibility of NTSB reports in civil suits)

provenance.generation_rule: Conceptual knowledge card: question derived from NTSB investigative methodology; answer constructed from regulatory and procedural sources.

prompt: Describe the ICAO Annex 13 phases of flight used for accident classification. Why does flight phase matter in accident analysis?
gold_decision: ICAO Annex 13 classifies accidents by phase of flight: (1) Standing — aircraft stationary, engines on/off; (2) Pushback/Tow; (3) Taxi; (4) Takeoff — from brake release to 35 ft AGL or V1; (5) Initial climb — 35 ft to transition altitude; (6) Enroute — cruise phase; (7) Maneuvering — intentional low-altitude flight, aerobatics; (8) Approach — instrument or visual approach; (9) Landing — touchdown and rollout; (10) Other (including ditching, emergency descent). Flight phase matters because: (a) different hazard profiles exist per phase (takeoff/approach = highest risk), (b) crew workload and automation usage differ, (c) regulatory standards differ (e.g. CAT I vs CAT II minima), (d) phase determines which causal chains to examine (e.g. windshear on approach vs. structural failure in cruise), and (e) safety recommendations are phase-specific.
required_safety_constraints: Approximately 70% of fatal accidents occur in takeoff, initial climb, approach, and landing phases combined
ICAO Annex 13 applies to accidents to aircraft of contracting states; 49 CFR Part 830 covers NTSB jurisdiction
acceptable_variants: Answer may use NTSB CICTT phase taxonomy rather than pure ICAO Annex 13 — both are acceptable
Minor variations in phase names are acceptable if conceptually correct

provenance.generation_rule: Conceptual knowledge card: flight phase taxonomy derived from ICAO Annex 13 and NTSB CICTT coding taxonomy.

prompt: Explain the HFACS (Human Factors Analysis and Classification System) framework. What are the four levels of HFACS and how do they relate to the Swiss Cheese Model?
gold_decision: HFACS has four hierarchical levels: (1) Unsafe Acts — errors (skill-based, decision, perceptual) and violations (routine, exceptional) by the operators (crew). (2) Preconditions for Unsafe Acts — adverse mental/physical states (complacency, channelized attention, fatigue, hypoxia), adverse physiological states, physical/mental limitations; crew resource management failures; environmental factors (operational/physical environment). (3) Unsafe Supervision — inadequate supervision, planned inappropriate operations, failure to correct known problems, supervisory violations. (4) Organizational Influences — resource management (human, monetary, equipment), organizational climate, operational process. Relation to Swiss Cheese: each HFACS level represents a 'slice' of cheese; holes (failures) at each level must align for an accident to occur. The model moves from the sharp end (pilot actions) to the blunt end (organization), reflecting Reason's original insight that accidents are system-level failures, not just operator errors.
required_safety_constraints: HFACS does not assign blame — it is a safety analysis framework
A finding at the unsafe acts level does not preclude finding failures at higher levels
HFACS requires trained raters for reliable classification
acceptable_variants: Answer may list sub-categories differently as long as four main levels are correct
Answer may reference Wiegmann & Shappell (2003) as HFACS originators

provenance.generation_rule: Human factors knowledge card: HFACS framework description synthesized from Wiegmann & Shappell (2003) and Reason (1990); no scenario data.

prompt: What is the SHELL model in aviation human factors? Describe each component and its relevance to accident analysis.
gold_decision: The SHELL model (Hawkins 1987, elaborated by Edwards) has five components: (S) Software — procedures, checklists, rules, training programs, publications; (H) Hardware — aircraft design, controls, instruments, seating, displays; (E) Environment — physical (weather, lighting, noise, vibration) and organizational (corporate culture, regulatory context); (L-centre) Liveware (human) — the focal pilot/crew member being analyzed; (L-L interface) Liveware-Liveware — crew coordination, communication, CRM between humans. In accident analysis, the model directs investigators to examine mismatches at each interface: L-S (were procedures unclear?), L-H (did the design cause confusion?), L-E (did environment degrade performance?), L-L (was communication adequate?). A common finding is that accidents result from multiple interface mismatches, not a single human error.
required_safety_constraints: The SHELL model is analytical, not predictive — it cannot tell you which misfits will cause accidents
Software in SHELL means procedures, NOT computer software exclusively
acceptable_variants: Answer may place E as 'liveware-environment' interface rather than separate component — both conventions exist
Answer may use Edwards' formulation or Hawkins' formulation interchangeably

provenance.generation_rule: Human factors knowledge card: SHELL model description from ICAO Doc 9683 and Hawkins/Edwards foundational papers.

prompt: What constitutes 'substantial damage' to an aircraft under 49 CFR §830.2? Give three examples of damage that IS substantial and three that are NOT.
gold_decision: Substantial damage means damage or failure that adversely affects the structural strength, performance, or flight characteristics of the aircraft and would normally require major repair or replacement of the affected component. EXAMPLES THAT ARE SUBSTANTIAL: (1) engine mount structural failure; (2) fuselage skin cracks extending beyond pre-drilled stop holes; (3) landing gear strut collapse that bends the wing spar. EXAMPLES THAT ARE NOT SUBSTANTIAL (explicitly excluded by regulation): (1) engine failure or damage limited to the engine if only one engine fails/is damaged on a multi-engine aircraft; (2) bent fairings or cowlings; (3) small punctures leaving less than 30 cm holes; also excluded: ground damage to rotor/propeller blades, dents/scratches to skin, and damage to landing gear, wheels, tires, flaps, engine accessories, brakes, or wingtips, providing this damage does not affect structural strength or flight performance.
required_safety_constraints: 49 CFR §830.2 substantial damage exclusions are definitional, not discretionary
NTSB makes the final determination on whether damage is 'substantial'
acceptable_variants: Examples may differ as long as they correctly represent substantial vs. non-substantial
Answer may note that single-engine aircraft damage exclusion does not apply — only multi-engine exclusion for single engine failure

provenance.generation_rule: Regulatory knowledge card with examples: substantial damage definition and exclusions from 49 CFR §830.2.

prompt: What is Loss of Control In-Flight (LOC-I) and why is it the leading cause of fatal general aviation accidents? What causal factors are typically associated with LOC-I?
gold_decision: LOC-I is defined as a significant, unintended deviation from intended flight path, often resulting in an unrecoverable departure from controlled flight. LOC-I is the leading cause of fatal GA accidents because: (a) it has a very low survival rate once established; (b) it can occur rapidly, leaving insufficient recovery time; (c) it often occurs at low altitudes during critical phases (takeoff/approach). Typical causal factors: (1) Spatial disorientation — vestibular illusions in IMC leading pilot to believe level flight while in bank; (2) Stall/spin entry — most common in low-energy states (base-to-final turn, go-around with full flaps); (3) Pilot incapacitation — medical event removes aircraft control; (4) Upset during maneuvering flight — aerobatics, low-altitude turns; (5) Weather encounter — inadvertent IMC by VFR pilot; (6) Distraction — cockpit task saturation during critical phase; (7) Wake turbulence encounter — upset induced by preceding heavy aircraft.
required_safety_constraints: LOC-I recovery requires immediate and correct response; delayed recognition is often fatal
NTSB and FAA categorize LOC-I separately from CFIT — controlled flight into terrain is its own category
acceptable_variants: Answer may emphasize different causal factors as primary; LOC-I causation is multi-factorial
Stall/spin and spatial disorientation are the two most commonly cited — either may be listed first
evidence_requirements: FAA Safety Alert for Operators (SAFO) on LOC-I

provenance.generation_rule: Safety knowledge card: LOC-I definition and causal factors from NTSB accident statistics and FAA SAFO documentation.

prompt: Define Controlled Flight Into Terrain (CFIT) and explain the distinction between CFIT and LOC-I. What systems are designed to prevent CFIT?
gold_decision: CFIT is an airworthy, crew-controlled aircraft's unintentional contact with terrain, water, or an obstacle while the crew is unaware of the impending collision. Key distinction from LOC-I: in CFIT the crew is in full control of the aircraft — the aircraft is responding normally to crew inputs — but they are unaware of proximity to terrain. In LOC-I, control of the aircraft has been lost. CFIT prevention systems: (1) GPWS (Ground Proximity Warning System) — reactive, triggers on terrain-relative closure rate; Mode 1: excessive descent rate; Mode 2: excessive terrain closure; Mode 3: altitude loss after takeoff; Mode 4: unsafe terrain clearance; Mode 5: below glideslope; (2) TAWS (Terrain Awareness and Warning System) — proactive, uses GPS+terrain database to project future position; required for turbine aircraft >6 passengers (14 CFR §135.154); (3) Instrument approaches with DA/MDA — prevents descent below safe altitude; (4) MSAW (Minimum Safe Altitude Warning) — ATC tool; (5) Crew situational awareness training.
required_safety_constraints: Required for Part 135 turbine aircraft >6 passengers; not required for Part 91 GA unless voluntarily installed
GPWS is Mode 1-6; TAWS adds look-ahead terrain alerting — they are not synonymous
acceptable_variants: Answer may describe EGPWS (Enhanced GPWS = TAWS) instead of distinguishing GPWS/TAWS separately

provenance.generation_rule: Safety knowledge card: CFIT definition and prevention systems from FAA AC 23-18 and 14 CFR §135.154.

prompt: What is a 'runway excursion' in NTSB/ICAO accident taxonomy? Distinguish between a veer-off and an overrun, and identify the top three causal factors for each.
gold_decision: A runway excursion is departure of an aircraft from the runway surface during takeoff or landing roll. VEER-OFF: departure from the runway laterally (sideways off the side). Top three causes: (1) crosswind exceeding aircraft/pilot capability leading to directional control loss; (2) loss of directional control due to asymmetric thrust or brake failure; (3) hydroplaning on wet/contaminated runway reducing steering effectiveness. OVERRUN: departure from the runway end (front or back). Top three causes for landing overrun: (1) excessive approach speed or long touchdown point reducing available stopping distance; (2) runway surface contamination (wet, icy, snow-covered) reducing braking friction; (3) improper use of braking or thrust reversers. Top causes for takeoff overrun: (1) failure to abort at V1 when required; (2) failure of aircraft to accelerate to rotation speed (engine failure, density altitude); (3) contaminated runway reducing acceleration.
required_safety_constraints: Runway excursions account for approximately 25-30% of runway safety events globally (ICAO statistics)
Veer-off and overrun have different prevention strategies — conflating them leads to misdirected interventions
acceptable_variants: Answer may list causal factors in different order of priority
Answer may include runway length/slope as a factor for overruns

provenance.generation_rule: Operational knowledge card: runway excursion taxonomy from ICAO safety documents and FAA runway safety data.

prompt: What is the NTSB's maintenance-related accident categorization? Describe the difference between 'maintenance error' and 'design deficiency' as accident causes.
gold_decision: NTSB categorizes maintenance-related accidents under several findings categories: maintenance personnel issues (inspection errors, improper installation, failure to follow procedures, return-to-service errors); maintenance organization issues (inadequate maintenance program, inadequate oversight); airworthiness directive (AD) non-compliance; component failure without fault. MAINTENANCE ERROR: an act or omission by a maintenance technician that deviated from approved procedures and directly contributed to the accident. Examples: incorrect torque on fastener, wrong part installed, fuel cap left off, flight control not reconnected. DESIGN DEFICIENCY: an inherent flaw in the aircraft or component design that caused or contributed to the failure, independent of operator error. Examples: Cessna 172 carburetor heat known icing susceptibility, McDonnell Douglas DC-10 cargo door latch design (implicated in 1974 Paris crash). The distinction matters because design deficiencies may trigger airworthiness directives or fleet-wide action, while maintenance errors focus on training/procedural interventions.
required_safety_constraints: FAA ADs (Airworthiness Directives) are mandatory maintenance actions — non-compliance is a regulatory violation
Design deficiencies are typically identified through accident pattern analysis across multiple events
acceptable_variants: Answer may note that design deficiency findings are rare compared to maintenance error findings in NTSB reports

provenance.generation_rule: Maintenance knowledge card: maintenance error vs design deficiency taxonomy from NTSB investigative methodology.

prompt: Explain the 'chain of events' or 'accident chain' concept in aviation safety analysis. How does breaking the chain prevent accidents?
gold_decision: The accident chain is the sequential linking of causal and contributing events/factors that together produce an accident. No single factor is typically sufficient — the accident requires the chain to remain unbroken through multiple failures. Example chain: (1) pilot fatigued (precondition); (2) weather briefing incomplete (planning failure); (3) inadvertent IMC entry (initiating event); (4) spatial disorientation develops (deteriorating condition); (5) unusual attitude — LOC-I (accident). Breaking any link prevents the accident: (1) adequate rest; (2) thorough weather briefing; (3) VFR-only flight until IFR rated; (4) spatial disorientation awareness training; (5) upset recovery training. The model emphasizes that interventions at any point in the chain can prevent the outcome, and that blame allocation to the 'last link' (typically the pilot) ignores systemic preventive opportunities upstream.
required_safety_constraints: The accident chain model is analytical, not predictive — it cannot enumerate all possible chains in advance
ATC, maintenance, meteorology, and airspace design can each represent links in the chain
acceptable_variants: Answer may use James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model as the theoretical basis — both chain and cheese models are equivalent in this context
Answer may use a different example accident chain as illustration
evidence_requirements: Aviation safety literature on accident chain analysis

provenance.generation_rule: Safety theory knowledge card: accident chain concept from aviation safety literature and Reason's systemic accident model.

prompt: What are the NTSB Part 830 preservation of evidence requirements following an aircraft accident? What must the operator/owner NOT do until authorized by NTSB?
gold_decision: 49 CFR §830.10 requires: the operator (or owner if operator is unavailable) must preserve the wreckage, mail, cargo, and all records in its original condition to the maximum extent possible, pending authorization from the NTSB to do otherwise. MUST NOT (without NTSB authorization): (1) move or disturb the wreckage or any cargo or mail; (2) remove or alter any component of the aircraft; (3) destroy any records relevant to the accident. EXCEPTIONS: movement is permitted only to (a) remove persons injured or trapped; (b) protect the wreckage from further damage; (c) protect the public from injury. If the wreckage must be moved, the original position must be marked/documented first. The FAA also has separate rights of access to records under 49 USC §1132. Evidence preservation is mandatory, not optional, and failure to preserve can impede accident investigation findings.
required_safety_constraints: 49 CFR §830.10 preservation requirement is mandatory — violation may impede NTSB investigation
ATC recordings, CVR, and FDR preservation are covered by separate regulations (49 CFR Part 821 for ATC)
Insurers and legal counsel do NOT override NTSB evidence preservation authority
acceptable_variants: Answer may note that NTSB may authorize immediate release of wreckage in some cases (no fatalities, clear circumstances)

provenance.generation_rule: Regulatory knowledge card: 49 CFR §830.10 wreckage preservation requirements.

prompt: What is a Cockpit Voice Recorder (CVR) and Flight Data Recorder (FDR)? What are the regulatory requirements for their installation, and what is the '2-hour rule' for CVRs?
gold_decision: FDR: records specified aircraft parameters (altitude, airspeed, heading, vertical acceleration, pitch, roll, etc.) over at least the last 25 hours of operation. Required on: turbine-powered aircraft with 10+ passenger seats (14 CFR §135.152), large transport category aircraft. CVR: records cockpit area audio including crew communications, ATC communications, PA announcements, and area microphone audio. Required on: turbine-powered aircraft with 6+ passenger seats (crew of 2 required). THE '2-HOUR RULE' (14 CFR §23.1457/25.1457): the CVR must retain at least the last 2 hours of operation before the accident. Significance: manufacturers initially used 30-minute tape loops, which often erased critical pre-accident audio. After the 1996 ValuJet crash the FAA mandated 2-hour minimum retention. Modern CVRs retain ≥2 hours of digital audio. Both recorders must be crash-resistant (fire and impact) and must be deactivated within 5 minutes after landing in some configurations.
required_safety_constraints: CVR audio from cockpit communications is protected from public release under 49 USC §1154(a)(1) but transcript may be released
Post-accident, operators must preserve FDR/CVR — do NOT allow continued operation that would overwrite data
acceptable_variants: Answer may note specific 14 CFR part references for transport vs commuter category differences
2-hour rule may be stated as 'at least 2 hours' — some modern units retain longer

provenance.generation_rule: Regulatory knowledge card: CVR/FDR requirements from 14 CFR parts 23, 25, 135 and post-ValuJet history.

prompt: Explain the concept of 'correlation vs. causation' in NTSB accident analysis. Give an example of how a safety analyst could incorrectly attribute causation when only correlation exists.
gold_decision: Correlation means two variables co-occur or move together statistically, but causation means one variable directly produces the other. In accident analysis, establishing causation requires: temporal sequence (cause precedes effect), mechanism (plausible causal pathway), consistency (finding holds across multiple cases), ruling out confounders. INCORRECT CAUSATION EXAMPLE: An analyst reviews 50 LOC-I accidents and finds that 80% of pilots had their most recent flight review >11 months ago. The analyst concludes 'delayed flight review causes LOC-I.' However, correlation does not establish causation — pilots who are less active flyers may both delay flight reviews AND have more accidents due to lack of recency; the common cause (low flight hours) explains both variables. The correct finding is: 'lack of recent flight experience was a contributing factor' — not that the missing flight review itself caused the LOC-I. NTSB analysts avoid causal claims beyond what the evidence supports, using hedged language: 'consistent with,' 'appears to have,' 'could not be determined.'
required_safety_constraints: NTSB probable cause determinations use 'most likely cause' standard, not legal proof of causation
Safety recommendations must be supported by evidence-based causal or risk-reduction arguments, not correlation alone
acceptable_variants: Different valid correlation-causation example is acceptable
Answer may reference Bradford Hill criteria for causation

provenance.generation_rule: Analytical methods knowledge card: correlation vs causation in accident analysis, constructed from NTSB investigative principles.

prompt: What is a 'hard landing' in NTSB accident taxonomy? Under what conditions does a hard landing become reportable as an accident rather than a routine incident?
gold_decision: A hard landing is an abnormal runway contact where the vertical descent rate at touchdown significantly exceeds normal parameters, potentially causing structural damage or occupant injury. It becomes an NTSB-reportable ACCIDENT when: (1) any person suffers serious injury (hospitalization >48h, fracture other than simple finger/toe/nose, etc.); OR (2) the aircraft sustains substantial damage (adverse effect on structural strength, performance, or flight characteristics requiring major repair). It may remain a reportable INCIDENT (requiring NTSB notification) if neither threshold is met but the event was safety-significant. Typical threshold for hard landing damage inspection per manufacturer: vertical acceleration exceeding 2.0–2.5g depending on aircraft. The NTSB event code is 'Landing-flare/touchdown — Hard landing.' Contributing factors typically include: excessive sink rate on final, insufficient flare, gusty wind conditions, improper airspeed, student pilot inexperience. Note: landing gear strut collapse on a hard landing would likely be 'substantial damage.'
required_safety_constraints: Manufacturers specify hard landing inspection criteria in AMMs — inspection is mandatory before return to service
A hard landing resulting in no injury and only bent fairings/cowlings may NOT be a reportable accident per 49 CFR §830.2 exclusions
acceptable_variants: Answer may cite specific g-force thresholds for different aircraft types
Answer may note that Part 121/135 operators have additional internal reporting requirements beyond NTSB Part 830
evidence_requirements: Aircraft Maintenance Manual (AMM) hard landing inspection criteria

provenance.generation_rule: Operational/regulatory knowledge card: hard landing classification from 49 CFR §830.2 and NTSB event coding taxonomy.

prompt: What is spatial disorientation in aviation and why is it particularly dangerous at night or in IMC? Name three vestibular illusions that contribute to spatial disorientation accidents.
gold_decision: Spatial disorientation (SD) is the pilot's inability to correctly perceive the aircraft's position, attitude, or motion relative to Earth. It is dangerous at night/IMC because: (1) without visual reference, the pilot relies on vestibular (inner ear) and proprioceptive (body position) senses, which are unreliable for flight; (2) the illusions are compelling and often override cognitive recognition of the actual attitude shown on instruments; (3) correction requires disciplined instrument scanning against strong physiological urges. THREE VESTIBULAR ILLUSIONS: (1) The Graveyard Spiral — prolonged turn causes adaptation (vestibular signals cease), then level flight feels like opposite turn; pilot 'corrects' back into the original turn causing accelerating spiral descent. (2) The Leans — sudden level-off after gradual bank entry causes sensation of banking in opposite direction; pilot banks back toward the 'felt' level attitude. (3) Somatogravic Illusion — rapid forward acceleration (e.g., after engine-out go-around) feels like nose-high pitch; pilot pushes nose down into ground. This commonly causes accidents during go-around or after rejected takeoff.
required_safety_constraints: Spatial disorientation is implicated in approximately 15% of fatal GA accidents (NTSB statistics)
Type I SD: pilot unaware of disorientation; Type II: aware but unable to override; Type III: physical incapacitation by SD
acceptable_variants: Other valid illusions acceptable: Coriolis illusion, Inversion illusion, Elevator illusion, False horizon
Answer may define 'unrecognized' vs 'recognized' SD instead of Types I-III
evidence_requirements: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge (PHAK) Chapter 17

provenance.generation_rule: Human factors knowledge card: spatial disorientation from FAA PHAK Chapter 17 and vestibular physiology.

prompt: What is fuel exhaustion vs. fuel starvation as an aircraft accident cause? Why does this distinction matter for investigation and prevention?
gold_decision: FUEL EXHAUSTION: The aircraft has run completely out of usable fuel — total fuel quantity across all tanks is zero or below unusable. Engine stops due to absence of fuel. FUEL STARVATION: Fuel exists in the tanks but does not reach the engine(s). Causes: fuel selector on wrong/empty tank, fuel valve closed, fuel line blockage, vapor lock, engine-driven fuel pump failure with electric boost pump off. The distinction matters critically for investigation and prevention: EXHAUSTION causes: inadequate preflight fuel planning, failure to refuel, fuel gauge misread, unforeseen headwind extending flight time. Prevention: pre-flight fuel check to visual level + calculation, fuel reserves requirement (45 min day VFR, 30 min night VFR, 45 min IFR). STARVATION causes: procedural/mechanical — missing a fuel selector step, failure to switch tanks. Prevention: fuel management checklist discipline, tank-switching procedures, electric boost pump operation. In NTSB reports, distinguishing the cause determines whether the finding is 'improper fuel planning' (exhaustion) or 'improper fuel system management' (starvation) — different corrective actions.
required_safety_constraints: Fuel starvation is preventable with proper checklist use; it represents a procedural failure distinct from fuel planning
FAA requires minimum fuel reserves: 45 min VFR day, 30 min night, 45 min IFR (14 CFR §91.151, §91.167)
acceptable_variants: Answer may note vapor lock as a mechanical cause of starvation distinct from procedural starvation

provenance.generation_rule: Technical knowledge card: fuel exhaustion vs starvation definitions and prevention from FAA regulations and NTSB investigation taxonomy.

prompt: What is a 'fly-into-terrain' accident and how does the NTSB subcategorize these events? Distinguish between CFIT and Non-CFIT terrain contact.
gold_decision: Terrain contact events are divided into CFIT (Controlled Flight Into Terrain) and Non-CFIT categories. CFIT: airworthy aircraft under crew control makes unintentional ground contact while crew is unaware of proximity — the aircraft is flying normally but on a collision course with terrain/obstacle without the crew's knowledge. Example: IFR descent through incorrect altitude, VFR pilot in cloud hits mountainside. Non-CFIT terrain contact includes: (1) LOC-I with subsequent terrain impact — crew lost control, then hit terrain; terrain was not the hidden hazard, loss of control was; (2) Intentional descent to terrain (emergency forced landing — gear-up field landing); (3) Takeoff/departure phase CFIT — aircraft unable to climb over obstacle due to performance, density altitude, obstacle clearance miscalculation; (4) Runway excursion — departed runway and hit terrain/obstacle off-runway end. NTSB CICTT event codes: 'CFIT' is a specific event code; 'LOC-I with terrain impact' is coded as LOC-I. The subcategorization matters because CFIT implies navigation/awareness failure while LOC-I implies aircraft control failure — entirely different preventive strategies.
required_safety_constraints: TAWS/GPWS is specifically designed to prevent CFIT, not LOC-I
acceptable_variants: Answer may note that obstacle CFIT and terrain CFIT are sometimes tracked separately

provenance.generation_rule: Accident classification knowledge card: CFIT vs non-CFIT terrain contact taxonomy from ICAO and NTSB CICTT.

prompt: What is a Safety Management System (SMS) in aviation, and how does it relate to accident prevention? Name the four components of SMS.
gold_decision: A Safety Management System (SMS) is a formal, top-down, organization-wide approach to managing safety risk, encompassing policies, processes, and procedures for the systematic management of safety risk. It shifts the focus from reactive (accident investigation) to proactive (hazard identification) safety management. FOUR COMPONENTS (ICAO Annex 19 framework): (1) Safety Policy and Objectives — leadership commitment, safety accountabilities, safety goals, documentation; (2) Safety Risk Management — hazard identification, safety risk assessment (likelihood × severity matrix), risk mitigation and residual risk acceptance; (3) Safety Assurance — continuous monitoring of safety performance indicators (SPIs), internal safety audits, management of change; (4) Safety Promotion — safety training and education, communication of safety-critical information, safety culture development. SMS is required for Part 121 airlines (FAA final rule effective 2024), Part 145 repair stations (planned), and airport operators (FAA SMS rule). SMS does not replace reactive accident investigation but adds the proactive and predictive layers.
required_safety_constraints: SMS is mandatory for Part 121 certificate holders per 14 CFR Part 5 (effective March 2024)
SMS is a framework, not a guarantee of zero accidents — it is a risk reduction system
acceptable_variants: Answer may call component 2 'Safety Risk Management (SRM)' and component 3 'Safety Assurance (SA)' using ICAO acronyms

provenance.generation_rule: Safety management knowledge card: SMS four components from ICAO Annex 19 and 14 CFR Part 5.

prompt: In NTSB accident analysis, what is 'pilot incapacitation' and how is it typically identified post-accident? What are the three most common medical causes of in-flight pilot incapacitation?
gold_decision: Pilot incapacitation is defined as any physiological or psychological condition that prevents or impairs a pilot's ability to perform required flight duties. Post-accident identification is challenging because most incapacitation leaves no obvious cockpit evidence; investigators look for: autopilot disconnection logs, ATC voice recording of degraded speech or non-response, radar track deviations without apparent cause, toxicology results, medical history review, and absence of other causal factors. THREE MOST COMMON MEDICAL CAUSES: (1) Cardiac event (heart attack/arrhythmia) — the most common fatal incapacitation cause; sudden arrhythmia may produce immediate unconsciousness with no warning. (2) Hypoxia — especially in unpressurized aircraft above 10,000 ft without supplemental oxygen; insidious onset with euphoria before incapacitation; common in pressurization failure events. (3) Hypoglycemia (low blood sugar) — particularly in diabetic pilots who may have been improperly certified or had undisclosed diabetes; symptoms include confusion, unconsciousness. Others include stroke, carbon monoxide poisoning, and medication side effects.
required_safety_constraints: Dual-pilot operations provide redundancy — 14 CFR Part 121 requires two-pilot crews partly for incapacitation protection
FAA medical certification (first, second, third class) is designed to screen known risk factors but cannot prevent sudden cardiac events
acceptable_variants: Carbon monoxide poisoning may replace one of the three common causes — it is frequently cited
Answer may note that incapacitation findings are often 'probable' rather than 'certain' given post-mortem evidence limitations
evidence_requirements: FAA medical certification regulations (14 CFR Part 67)

provenance.generation_rule: Medical/human factors knowledge card: pilot incapacitation taxonomy from FAA medical literature and NTSB case studies.

prompt: What is the NTSB's Aviation Accident Database (CAROL)? What fields are typically available and what are its key limitations for research use?
gold_decision: CAROL (Case Analysis and Reporting Online) is the NTSB's public web-based interface to the aviation accident and incident database, providing access to case records from 1962 to present. KEY FIELDS AVAILABLE: accident number (NTSB report ID), event date and location, aircraft make/model/registration, injury severity (fatal/serious/minor/none), accident type (accident/incident), event/phase of flight codes (CICTT taxonomy), operator information, probable cause text, findings, safety recommendations, and for final reports: full narrative, analysis, meteorological data, wreckage information. KEY LIMITATIONS: (1) preliminary reports are incomplete — investigations may take months to years; (2) probable cause fields are only populated in final reports; (3) coding consistency varies by investigating office and time period; (4) does not include military aviation accidents (separate AFSAS); (5) foreign accidents may be incomplete (US investigative participation only); (6) free-text fields require NLP or manual review; (7) no direct link to CVR/FDR data.
required_safety_constraints: NTSB reports are investigative documents — not admissible as evidence of fault in civil proceedings (49 USC §1154)
Preliminary reports should not be used for final analysis — use final reports only
acceptable_variants: Answer may describe the flat-file CSV export format vs CAROL API access

provenance.generation_rule: Data source knowledge card: NTSB CAROL database description from NTSB public documentation and data.ntsb.gov.

prompt: What is the difference between a 'fatal accident rate' and a 'accident rate per flight hour' in aviation safety statistics? Why does GA appear much more dangerous than commercial air travel when measured per flight hour?
gold_decision: FATAL ACCIDENT RATE: number of fatal accidents per unit of exposure (typically per 100,000 flight hours or per 100,000 departures). This is the most meaningful safety metric. ACCIDENT RATE PER FLIGHT HOUR: total accidents (fatal + non-fatal) per unit of flight time — includes minor events and gives a broader picture of overall safety risk level. GA vs. COMMERCIAL COMPARISON: General aviation appears significantly more dangerous than commercial air transport measured per flight hour because: (1) GA pilots have vastly less training, recency, and proficiency than airline pilots; (2) GA aircraft lack redundancy (single engine, no TAWS, no FDR/CVR in most cases); (3) GA operations lack crew coordination (single-pilot most common); (4) GA aircraft operate in all weather/environments without the infrastructure support (dispatch, meteorology, maintenance) of Part 121; (5) GA includes training operations with high exposure to accidents (stalls, hard landings). Commercial aviation fatal accident rate is approximately 100–200x lower per flight hour than GA, though on a per-trip basis commercial aviation is even safer due to shorter average flight distances in GA.
required_safety_constraints: Statistics must specify exposure base (per hour vs per departure vs per passenger-mile) to be meaningful
Comparisons between GA and commercial aviation are not apple-to-apple due to vastly different operations, aircraft, and pilot populations
acceptable_variants: Exact ratio may vary; 100-200x lower is approximate — the specific number depends on year and data source cited

provenance.generation_rule: Statistical methods knowledge card: accident rate metrics and GA vs commercial comparison from NTSB/FAA annual statistics.

prompt: What is 'continuation bias' (or 'press-on-itis') and how has it been implicated in VFR-into-IMC accidents? What decision-making model is used to counter it?
gold_decision: Continuation bias (also called 'get-there-itis' or 'press-on-itis') is the cognitive tendency to continue a planned course of action despite accumulating evidence that it should be abandoned. In aviation, it manifests as pressing on into deteriorating weather despite warning signs. VFR-INTO-IMC MECHANISM: a VFR pilot departs in marginal conditions; weather deteriorates gradually; each step of deterioration seems acceptable because the last step was survived; spatial disorientation, LOC-I, or CFIT ultimately results. The gradual deterioration makes each individual decision seem reasonable, even as the cumulative path leads to danger. NTSB finds continuation bias in approximately 80% of VFR-into-IMC fatal accidents. COUNTER-DECISION MODEL: The DECIDE model (Detect, Estimate, Choose, Identify, Do, Evaluate) and the 'Five Hazardous Attitudes' (anti-authority, impulsivity, invulnerability, macho, resignation) framework are FAA-endorsed models for countering bias. The primary mitigation is pre-set personal minimums with pre-committed turn-back criteria — decisions made on the ground before flight, not in-flight when bias is strongest.
required_safety_constraints: Pre-set personal minimums must be established before flight — in-flight decisions are compromised by continuation bias
IMC without an instrument rating is a regulatory violation (14 CFR §61.3) as well as a safety violation
acceptable_variants: Answer may use 'get-there-itis' as the colloquial term equivalently
DECIDE model or 3P model (Perceive-Process-Perform) are both acceptable counter-frameworks

provenance.generation_rule: Human factors knowledge card: continuation bias and VFR-into-IMC accident patterns from FAA Risk Management Handbook and NTSB studies.

prompt: What is a 'Part 91 personal flight' vs a 'Part 91 instructional flight' in NTSB accident records? Why does the operating category matter for safety analysis?
gold_decision: PART 91 PERSONAL FLIGHT: operated under 14 CFR Part 91 for private, non-commercial purposes; pilot typically flying for recreation, transportation, or personal business; no compensation involved. PART 91 INSTRUCTIONAL FLIGHT: operated under Part 91 with a flight instructor providing dual instruction to a student or trainee; may use a pilot school's aircraft; the flight is still general aviation but the dual-instruction nature creates different risk profiles. The operating category matters for safety analysis because: (1) accident rates, causes, and recommended interventions differ significantly between subcategories; (2) instructional accidents cluster around approach/landing (student error in flare) and takeoff/initial climb phases; personal flights cluster more around weather-related LOC-I and VFR-into-IMC; (3) safety recommendations must be targeted — improving instructor oversight addresses instructional accidents but not personal flight VFR-into-IMC; (4) Part 121 (airline) and Part 135 (charter/commuter) have additional regulations and oversight absent from Part 91, making Part 91 the highest-risk operational category.
required_safety_constraints: Part 91 flight operations are not subject to the same dispatch, weather minimums, or crew rest rules as Part 121 or Part 135
Flight instruction under Part 91 requires a certificated flight instructor (CFI) — 14 CFR §61.193
acceptable_variants: Answer may note Part 91 subcategories beyond personal and instructional: business, positioning, ferry flights
evidence_requirements: NTSB accident database coding for flight purpose

provenance.generation_rule: Regulatory context knowledge card: Part 91 flight purpose categories and their safety analysis implications.

prompt: What is the 'tombstone imperative' in aviation safety and how does the modern SMS proactive approach challenge this model?
gold_decision: The 'tombstone imperative' (also 'tombstone regulation') refers to the historical pattern in aviation safety regulation where significant regulatory change or safety improvement follows only after a fatal accident — typically a high-visibility disaster — rather than being proactively implemented on the basis of hazard analysis alone. Historically, many major FAA safety rules were enacted in response to specific catastrophic accidents: after the 1956 Grand Canyon mid-air collision (ATC expansion), after the 1977 Tenerife disaster (standardized ATC phraseology push), after the 1988 Aloha Airlines fuselage failure (aging aircraft inspection programs). The modern SMS proactive challenge: SMS explicitly requires ongoing hazard identification, risk assessment, and mitigation BEFORE accidents occur. Safety performance indicators (SPIs) track leading indicators (e.g., go-around rate, GPWS activations) rather than just lagging indicators (accident rate). Safety Assurance audits detect systemic issues before they produce accidents. The shift is from reactive tombstone-driven regulation to proactive risk management — reducing reliance on accidents as the primary driver of safety improvements.
required_safety_constraints: SMS does not eliminate accident investigation — reactive analysis remains essential for learning from events that do occur
acceptable_variants: Answer may use different historical accident examples as long as the tombstone pattern is correctly described

provenance.generation_rule: Safety culture knowledge card: tombstone imperative vs proactive SMS from aviation safety management literature.

prompt: What is 'wire strike' as an accident category? Which flight operations are at highest risk and what regulatory/equipment mitigations exist?
gold_decision: Wire strike is in-flight collision with power lines, telephone wires, fences, or other cable infrastructure. It is a significant cause of helicopter fatal accidents and low-altitude fixed-wing accidents. HIGHEST RISK OPERATIONS: (1) Helicopter EMS (HEMS) — nighttime operations, unfamiliar terrain, pressure to complete missions; (2) Agricultural/aerial application — extremely low altitude operations (10–15 ft AGL) in areas with power lines near crop rows; (3) Aerial survey/patrol — low-level terrain-following along power lines (the ultimate irony — power line inspection creates wire strike risk); (4) Training flights with low-altitude maneuvering; (5) Emergency off-airport landings — descending toward unmapped wire strikes. REGULATORY/EQUIPMENT MITIGATIONS: (1) Wire strike protection systems (WSPS) on helicopters — cutter blades above and below the fuselage on military and some EMS helicopters; (2) NVG (Night Vision Goggles) for HEMS operations — FAA HEMS rule (14 CFR §135.609) effective 2014 requires risk assessment program; (3) VFR sectional chart wire markings (major lines only — local distribution lines not charted); (4) Helicopter terrain awareness and warning system (HTAWS) — adapts TAWS for low-altitude helicopter operations; (5) HEMS risk assessment programs.
required_safety_constraints: Wire strikes are particularly lethal because wires are not charted on VFR sectionals at distribution voltage levels
HEMS operators must have FAA-approved risk assessment programs under 14 CFR §135.609 effective 2014
acceptable_variants: Answer may add crop-dusting statistics or mention the Bell 407/206 wire cutter certification

provenance.generation_rule: Operational knowledge card: wire strike accident category from NTSB data and 14 CFR §135.609 HEMS requirements.

prompt: Under ICAO Annex 13, who has jurisdiction to lead an aircraft accident investigation — the State of Occurrence, the State of Registry, or the State of the Operator? Explain the roles of each.
gold_decision: Under ICAO Annex 13 Chapter 5, the STATE OF OCCURRENCE has the primary responsibility to institute an investigation. If the accident occurs over the high seas or territory not belonging to any state, the State of Registry takes responsibility. ROLES: STATE OF OCCURRENCE — institutes and leads the investigation; notifies the State of Registry, State of the Operator, State of Design, State of Manufacture, and ICAO; may delegate the investigation to another state. STATE OF REGISTRY — accredited representative participates in the investigation; has the right to appoint advisers. STATE OF THE OPERATOR — accredited representative participates; right to appoint advisers; most directly interested in operational findings. STATE OF DESIGN/MANUFACTURE — accredited representation, especially relevant if airworthiness issues are found. In practice, for US-registered aircraft with US carriers operating in the US: NTSB (State of Occurrence = State of Registry = State of Operator) leads the investigation with no conflicts. International complications arise when, e.g., a French-designed Airbus, registered in Germany, operated by a UK carrier, crashes in Spain.
required_safety_constraints: The investigating state must notify ICAO of accidents to aircraft >2,250 kg max certificated weight (Annex 13, Chapter 4)
Accredited representatives have defined rights under Annex 13 — they are not observers
acceptable_variants: Answer may note the US-ICAO relationship — NTSB fulfills US obligations under Annex 13

provenance.generation_rule: International regulatory knowledge card: ICAO Annex 13 investigation jurisdiction from Chapter 5 and 6.

prompt: What is 'birdstrike' as an NTSB accident/incident category? What makes a birdstrike an accident vs. an incident, and what reporting mechanism exists outside Part 830?
gold_decision: A birdstrike (wildlife strike) is a collision between an aircraft and one or more birds or other wildlife during flight operations. Under 49 CFR Part 830, a birdstrike becomes an ACCIDENT only if it produces serious injury or substantial aircraft damage. Most birdstrikes are incidents or unreportable occurrences — engine FOD without structural damage, windshield crack without pressurization failure, etc. However, a birdstrike that ingests into both engines and causes dual flameout (like US Airways 1549, 2009) producing an emergency water landing IS an accident. SEPARATE REPORTING MECHANISM: The FAA Wildlife Strike Database (FAA Form 5200-7) is a voluntary reporting system separate from NTSB Part 830. Operators, pilots, and airports can report wildlife strikes to the FAA, which maintains the database for trend analysis. It is NOT mandatory for most strikes — only strikes involving substantial damage or injury trigger Part 830 notification. The voluntary database captures far more events than mandatory reporting and provides data for bird hazard assessment at airports. Airport wildlife management plans are required at certificated airports (14 CFR Part 139).
required_safety_constraints: FAA Form 5200-7 reporting is voluntary for most strikes — it is NOT a replacement for mandatory NTSB Part 830 reporting where applicable
14 CFR Part 139 airport certification requires wildlife hazard management plans at certificated airports
acceptable_variants: Answer may cite the FAA wildlife strike database (BASH) by its informal acronym

provenance.generation_rule: Regulatory knowledge card: birdstrike accident/incident classification and FAA voluntary wildlife database from FAA Form 5200-7 and 14 CFR Part 139.

prompt: What is 'aerodynamic stall' as an accident cause, and how does it differ from a 'spin'? At what phase of flight are stall/spin accidents most prevalent and most fatal?
gold_decision: AERODYNAMIC STALL: condition in which the wing's angle of attack (AoA) exceeds the critical AoA, causing sudden separation of airflow over the upper wing surface and a significant decrease in lift. The stall is an aerodynamic condition, not a speed condition per se — it can occur at any airspeed if AoA is exceeded (accelerated stall). SPIN: a stall combined with yaw, producing an autorotation condition where one wing is more stalled than the other, creating an oscillating, descending rotation. Spins are possible following stalls if rudder/aileron input creates asymmetric stall. PHASE OF FLIGHT: Stall/spin accidents are most prevalent and most fatal during: (1) BASE-TO-FINAL TURN — cross-control stall (opposite aileron, same-direction rudder) at low altitude; most lethal because altitude is insufficient for recovery; (2) INITIAL CLIMB (takeoff) — engine failure with improper response leads to stall during slow-flight attempted return to runway ('impossible turn'); (3) GO-AROUND — adding full power with full flaps at slow speed creates pitch-up tendency; improper correction leads to stall. High-altitude stalls in cruise (e.g., AF 447 type) are less common in GA.
required_safety_constraints: Stall recovery altitude requirement: stall must be recognized and recovery initiated before loss of controlled flight — no minimum altitude guarantee
Cross-control stall at low altitude is particularly insidious — banking toward the runway to correct for overshoot of final is a common fatal error
acceptable_variants: Answer may specify that spin recovery requires specific training (endorsement required per 14 CFR §61.183 for CFI)
AF 447 example may or may not be used — it is a high-altitude stall not representative of GA pattern

provenance.generation_rule: Aerodynamic/accident knowledge card: stall vs spin definition and phase-of-flight prevalence from FAA AFH and NTSB stall/spin study.

prompt: What is the role of a 'safety recommendation' in NTSB accident reports? Can the NTSB compel action on its recommendations?
gold_decision: NTSB safety recommendations are formal written communications from the NTSB to agencies, organizations, or individuals identifying corrective actions to address safety deficiencies identified in accident investigations. They are among the most important outputs of NTSB investigations. LEGAL STATUS: The NTSB CANNOT compel action on its recommendations — it has no regulatory authority. The NTSB is an independent federal agency that investigates and recommends, but cannot regulate, mandate, or enforce. The FAA, DOT, DOD, manufacturers, and operators are the recipients who may or may not act on recommendations. TRACKING: The NTSB tracks recommendation responses and classifies them as: Open-Acceptable Response, Open-Unacceptable Response, Closed-Acceptable Action, Closed-Unacceptable Action, or Superseded. Recommendations that remain unaddressed over years appear on the NTSB 'Most Wanted List' (MWL) of transportation safety improvements — a public advocacy tool.
required_safety_constraints: NTSB 'Most Wanted List' is a public advocacy tool, not a regulatory enforcement mechanism
49 USC §1135 defines NTSB authority to make safety recommendations — enforcement authority is expressly absent
acceptable_variants: Answer may note that despite lack of enforcement authority, NTSB recommendations have a high historical implementation rate (~80%) due to institutional credibility

provenance.generation_rule: Regulatory/institutional knowledge card: NTSB safety recommendation authority and process from 49 USC §1135.

prompt: What is the 'dirty dozen' in aviation maintenance human factors? List five of the twelve factors.
gold_decision: The 'Dirty Dozen' is a framework developed by Transport Canada identifying twelve human factors that most commonly contribute to maintenance errors. The concept is used in aviation maintenance resource management (MRM) training. FIVE KEY FACTORS from the twelve: (1) Lack of Communication — maintenance personnel failing to document or verbally hand off work in progress; improperly completed maintenance documentation; (2) Complacency — over-confidence in familiar tasks, reduction in vigilance on routine inspections, 'I've done this 100 times'; (3) Lack of Knowledge — performing tasks beyond training/experience, insufficient technical documentation available, inadequate training on new systems; (4) Distraction — interruptions during critical assembly steps causing loss of place in the checklist, foreign object debris (FOD) from distracted work; (5) Fatigue — impaired judgment and attention from extended work hours, night shifts, compressed schedules. The remaining seven: lack of resources, pressure (time/production), lack of assertiveness, stress, lack of awareness, norms (shortcuts accepted as normal), and lack of teamwork.
required_safety_constraints: The Dirty Dozen are contributing factors, not causes — addressing them requires systemic changes, not just individual awareness
MRM training using the Dirty Dozen framework is required for Part 145 repair stations in some countries (Transport Canada, EASA)
acceptable_variants: Answer may list any five of the twelve correctly
Answer may vary slightly in naming convention — exact names vary by source
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 120-72 Maintenance Resource Management Training

provenance.generation_rule: Maintenance human factors knowledge card: Dirty Dozen framework from Transport Canada MRM documentation and FAA AC 120-72.

prompt: What is 'engine failure on takeoff' (EFATO) and why is the decision altitude/speed critical? What is the 'impossible turn' and why does it kill pilots?
gold_decision: EFATO (Engine Failure After Takeoff) is loss of engine power following brake release during the takeoff roll or after liftoff, before reaching a safe altitude for emergency options. The critical decision point is defined differently by aircraft: for twin-engine aircraft, it is VMC (minimum controllable airspeed) and VYSE (best single-engine rate-of-climb speed); for single-engine aircraft, the pilot has essentially one option: land more-or-less straight ahead. THE 'IMPOSSIBLE TURN': the temptation — when an engine fails shortly after takeoff — to turn 180° back to the departure runway rather than land straight ahead. It is called 'impossible' because: (1) the minimum altitude to complete a 180° turn and align with the runway exceeds what most pilots believe (typically 1,000+ ft AGL for a typical light aircraft); (2) pilots significantly underestimate the altitude loss in the banked turn at slow speed; (3) initiating a steep bank at low airspeed risks an accelerated stall/spin while distracted; (4) pilots often turn into the turn (toward the runway) instead of away, requiring a >180° total turn with more altitude loss. The FAA recommends landing straight ahead or with minimal deviation up to 500 ft AGL in most training guidance.
required_safety_constraints: The turn-back altitude varies by aircraft type and bank angle — there is no universal rule; pilots should know their specific aircraft's numbers
Training for EFATO should include personal altitude minimums established before flight, not improvised in the emergency
acceptable_variants: Answer may cite specific altitude minimums from training literature — these vary by source and aircraft
evidence_requirements: FAA Airplane Flying Handbook on EFATO

provenance.generation_rule: Operational knowledge card: EFATO and impossible turn from FAA AFH and NTSB EFATO accident analysis.

prompt: Under 49 CFR Part 830, how long must operators wait after an accident before moving the wreckage without NTSB authorization, assuming no exceptional circumstances?
gold_decision: There is no specified waiting period in 49 CFR Part 830. The regulation (§830.10) requires preservation of the wreckage until the NTSB authorizes its disturbance or removal. This means the operator must contact the NTSB and obtain authorization before moving the wreckage — there is no automatic release after a specified number of hours or days. The NTSB investigator will determine when wreckage may be moved, which can range from same-day authorization (for minor accidents with no fatalities and clear circumstances) to weeks or months for complex fatal accidents. The only exception is emergency humanitarian movement to (a) remove injured/trapped persons, (b) protect the wreckage from further damage, or (c) protect the public from injury — in which case the original position must be documented before movement.
required_safety_constraints: 49 CFR §830.10 has NO automatic time-release provision — NTSB authorization is required regardless of time elapsed
Common misconception: some pilots believe wreckage can be moved after 48 or 72 hours — this is incorrect
acceptable_variants: Answer may note that for NTSB field investigations, the investigator-in-charge typically releases wreckage quickly for minor accidents

provenance.generation_rule: Regulatory knowledge card: 49 CFR §830.10 wreckage preservation — testing misconception about automatic time-release.
